blocks.text: The Doctrine and Covenants, 1844

The demand for scriptures skyrocketed in the six years after publication of the first edition of the Doctrine and Covenants. That demand was driven in large part by the growth of the Church. In 1835, the Church had been largely an American institution, but by 1841, waves of converts from Canada and England helped increase membership numbers after the divisive apostasy in Kirtland. Among those converted in Canada was a lay preacher named John Taylor, who became a member of the Church’s Quorum of the Twelve Apostles and would one day lead the Church. He would also play a role in the publication of the 1844 second edition of the Doctrine and Covenants.

Parley P. Pratt, the missionary who taught John Taylor, went with him and other members of the Quorum of the Twelve to England in fulfillment of a revelation Joseph Smith had received in 1838. In New York, on his way to the British Isles, Parley wrote to Joseph Smith, declaring that there was “a great call for our Books” among the Church members in the eastern United States.

That same demand for scriptures and other Church books was felt at the Church’s new home in Nauvoo, Illinois, where Church members scattered by the Kirtland apostasy and the Missouri persecutions were now gathering. Ebenezer Robinson, who published the third edition of the Book of Mormon with Joseph Smith’s permission in 1840 and a Church hymnal in March 1841, now turned his sights to the Doctrine and Covenants.

Ebenezer’s growing print operation needed a larger home, and in November 1841, he moved across the street into a building that housed additional facilities he needed for publishing books: a bindery and a stereotype foundry for making plates from type that had been set, thereby allowing for easy reprinting of books. About the time he moved into the new print shop, he began setting type for the second edition of the Doctrine and Covenants.

Ebenezer Robinson’s zeal to publish Church books raised fears that he might become commercially proprietary about works that were spiritual in nature. Concerned, Joseph Smith sought and received revelation directing that the printing operation be placed under the control of members of the Quorum of the Twelve, who had been given increasing authority since 1841 when most members of the quorum returned to Nauvoo after their mission to England. To be fair to Ebenezer, Joseph Smith purchased the printing operation from him for $6,600.

John Taylor and Wilford Woodruff were put in charge of the print shop under Joseph’s guidance. Later, in January 1844, Joseph turned the printing operation over to John Taylor, allowing Wilford Woodruff to attend to other duties.

During the time he worked on the Doctrine and Covenants, Ebenezer Robinson typeset and stereotyped the first quarter of the book. Delayed by other responsibilities, Elders Taylor and Woodruff took until the end of 1843 to finish most of the rest. Meanwhile, Joseph Smith and William W. Phelps helped proofread the type as it was set. With the typesetting stage nearing completion, Joseph ordered paper for the printing.

By late spring of 1844, the book was nearly ready to be distributed. On June 12, 1844, the Nauvoo Neighbor newspaper published a notice that read, “The Book of Doctrine and Covenants will be published in about one month from this time. Those wishing for an early supply had better make immediate application.” The book, it seemed, would soon be in circulation.

Storm clouds, however, were about to burst on Nauvoo. Just five days before this notice appeared in the Nauvoo Neighbor, the first and only issue of the Nauvoo Expositor newspaper appeared in the city, sparking a series of events that led to Joseph Smith’s turning himself over to government authorities at Carthage, Illinois, the county seat. Relying on a pledge of protection from the governor of the state, Joseph waited in the Carthage Jail with his brother Hyrum, Church historian Willard Richards, and John Taylor, the printer who was responsible for completing the publication of the Doctrine and Covenants.

On June 27, a mob stormed the jail and fired on the Church leaders, who rushed toward the door of their room to brace it against the press of their attackers. Lead balls pierced the air as smoke billowed from the barrels of guns, accompanied by the flash of exploding power. Outnumbered by as many as fifty to one, with just two small guns and a couple of canes to defend themselves, Joseph and his associates found themselves hopelessly trapped.

Hyrum fell first, struck in the face by a ball that splintered through the wooden door. As the door was forced open and gun barrels belched their deadly loads, John Taylor and Joseph Smith in turn dashed for the east window of the room. Both were cut down by gunfire, John falling to the floor and being hit repeatedly, Joseph tumbling out the second-story window after being struck by gunfire from both inside and outside the jail.

Someone shouted that Joseph had leaped out the window, and the mob against the door withdrew, leaving Willard Richards (who had been grazed on the left ear) alone in the room with two of his friends, one dead, another wounded. “The Mormons are coming!” someone cried in false alarm, and the vigilantes scattered, leaving their bullet-ridden victims behind.

To Willard, the historian, fell the responsibility of reporting what had happened. He penned a brief message to Nauvoo. “Joseph and Hyrum are dead,” the note began, “Taylor wounded, not very badly.” John Taylor bravely cosigned the letter, not wanting his family to be alarmed, but in fact his wounds were ghastly.

One “ball from the door” hit him “about midway of my thigh,” he later wrote. It “struck the bone, and flattened out almost to the size of a quarter of a dollar, and then passed on through the fleshy part to within about half an inch of the outside.” Another ball “entered a little below the left knee,” and yet another entered “the forepart of my left arm, a little above the wrist, and passing down by the joint, lodged in the fleshy part of my hand, about midway, a little above the upper joint of my little finger.” A fourth ball “struck me on the fleshy part of my left hip, and tore away the flesh as large as my hand, dashing the mangled fragments of flesh and blood against the wall.”

A short time after the shooting, as John Taylor lay on the floor at the top of the stairs, a local doctor pulled a “dull penknife” from his pocket and sawed “for some time” on Elder Taylor’s left hand till he had opened it up. Using “a pair of carpenter’s compasses”—but nothing by way of anesthesia in those days—the doctor pried until he extracted the half-ounce ball. Other than that act of “surgical butchery,” as John Taylor described it, he received no real first aid, though he was moved to a more comfortable location. “I lay from about five o’clock until two the next morning without having my wounds dressed,” he wrote, since nearly all the citizens of Carthage had fled after the attack.

The day after the shootings, another doctor extracted the ball from John Taylor’s now very swollen left thigh. “The doctor asked me if I would be tied during the operation,” Elder Taylor wrote. “I told him no.”

During the days after the murders, danger still remained from those who had participated in the crime or condoned it. Some in the area said John Taylor too “ought to be killed, and they would do it, but that it was too damned cowardly to shoot a wounded man.” By the time friends had gathered to transport him back to Nauvoo, “the loss of blood and the great discharge” from his wounds had left him so weak that when his wife asked if he could talk, he “could barely whisper no.”

After reaching Nauvoo and recovering somewhat, John Taylor returned to the print shop, where the second edition of the Doctrine and Covenants had been largely typeset and stereotyped. As it was set, the book would have had blank space at the end of the final section. Using this space—and much smaller type than for the revelations—he and his associates added a tribute to the fallen martyrs.

Most of the 1844 Doctrine and Covenants simply reprinted the contents of the first edition of 1835. It included the seven theological lectures and the 103 revelations from that edition. But it also included seven additional items from Joseph Smith and the final tribute to him.
“To seal the testimony of this book and the Book of Mormon,” the tribute began, “we close with the martyrdom of Joseph Smith the prophet, and Hyrum Smith the patriarch.”

After describing the murders of these men, the tribute lists Joseph Smith’s accomplishments, which included bringing forth “the revelations and commandments which compose this book of Doctrine and Covenants, and many other wise documents and instructions for the benefit of the children of men.”

Near the end, the tribute declared of Joseph and Hyrum, “Hence forward their names will be classed among the martyrs of religion: and the reader in every nation, will be reminded that the ‘Book of Mormon’ and this book of Doctrine and Covenants of the church, cost the best blood of the nineteenth century, to bring it forth for the salvation of a ruined world.”

Salvation was the ultimate purpose of the Doctrine and Covenants and of Joseph Smith’s mission as prophet, seer, and revelator. During one of the Prophet’s darkest hours, the Lord declared to him, “Thine adversity and thine afflictions shall be but a small moment.”
That moment was over, and it was now up to others to take the revelations of God to the world through the pages of the Doctrine and Covenants.
